description: Measure from bank to bank and the chosen side-to-side extent is the river's 'lonai', its width. Doorways, cloth, and paths have the same dimension before anyone judges them wide or narrow. 'losha' and 'hieru' make that judgment; 'waleru' follows end-to-end extent and 'theru' thickness between opposing surfaces.
articulatory_notes: Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for lateral 'l', pass the air around its sides, and round into middle-height back 'o'. Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for dental 'n' and open into stressed central 'a'. Keep the final high-front 'i' separate across the hiatus.
usage_notes: The thing being gauged comes first, as in 'wetha lonai' (the cloth's width). The chosen sides, exact value, and unit remain with the measurement record when they matter.

examples.translation: The two pieces of cloth have equal width.

semantic_domains.physical: side-to-side extent across an object, opening, path, or body of water
semantic_domains.quantity: a dimension available for comparison or measurement
